meaning: to, toward, for, to the advantage of, with regard to, according to, of (as in bet-u la-nəgus 'the king's house', lit. 'his-house of-(or to) the-king'; for more details, see Dillmann 1907: 391-2; Lambdin 44-5); it indicates purpose: e.g. šannāy la bali 'good to eat'; it serves for the expression of the direct complement (see Dillmann 1907:391); la- prefixed to the jussive expresses a wish, a command or an injunction; e.g. la-yəkun bərhan 'let there be light!'; for la- with the suffix pronouns, see Dillmann 1907:406; see also lita. lalla- (repeated la) expresses the distributive, '(at, for) every, each'; e.g. lalla ba'āl 'at every feast'; with suffix pronouns the base is lalli-, lalla-; e.g. lallāya 'for my part', lallihu 'he himself, he alone', and so on (see Dillmann 1907:344, 374).
origin_explanation: SEMITIC and ETHIOPIC: la, li, lə, Te. əl. For this particle in Semitic, see Brockelmann 1913:377ff; in Ethiopic, see Leslau 1956:51, 149. For the expression of the distributive, see Dillmann 1907:373-5.

meaning: 'messenger, servant, attend ant, deacon, minister, apostle'
messenger, apostle, cantor (ecclesias tic) of lower rank'
'messenger, angel, gover nor, prefect, prince, chief, captain, ruler, commander'
'letter, message, epistle, duty, busi ness, service, cult, mission, ministry, function, office, legation'

origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. la'afa 'eat becomingly', Soq. ləf 'eat'. ETHIOPIC: Amh. lafä 'swallow without chewing, eat quickly'; also in Cushitic: Som. Af. la'af 'bait', Sa. lä'if.

meaning: 'be sent, serve (lit. 'be sent'), assist, be employed, minister, administer'

origin_explanation: misprint for lasd

meaning: talä'ala 'rise, rise up, raise oneself, be raised, be lifted, ascend, be superior, be exalted, be extolled'

origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. labaḥa 'mix' (which is done by touch-ing), Aram.-Syr. labaḥ 'lay hold of, hold fast'. ETHIOPIC: Te. labbaḥa 'rub in'.

meaning: 'storax tree'; (māya labn 'storax', lit. 'water [sap] of the storax tree');
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. labnā 'storax', Sar. lbny, Heb. libnē 'storax tree'. CUSHITIC: Af. lawin, lauwīn.

meaning: 'kind of tree'; T renders it by Amh. lambāč 'a kind of palm tree'; KG renders it by Amh. lammāč 'a kind of tree whose twigs are used as toothbrushes'.

'clothe oneself, put on clothes, wear'; also 'clothe' (e.g. labsa lə-'adām 'he clothed Adam'); 'əlbasa 'clothe, dress'; tǝlabsa, passive, reflexive; tǝlabäsa 'disguise oneself, put on ostentatious clothes'; 'əstǝlabäsa (Gr.29) 'clothe several persons'; labäsi, act. part. of labsa; läbs (fem. läbsǝt) 'dressed, clothed'; läbs (pl. 'əlbäs, läbsät, 'əlbäsät) 'clothes, gar-ment, apparel, tunic, curtain (of court), silk veil of oblation in the church'; läbsat (n.) 'dressing, clothing'; malbas, malbast (pl. maläläbs) 'clothing, gar-ment, tunic, cover';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. labisa 'wear, put on a garment', Sar. lbs, Mh. lbes, Heb. läbeš, Aram.-Syr. lǝbeš, Md.Ug. lbs, Akk. labāšu. ETHIOPIC: Te. läbsa 'dress', Tna. gur. läbäsa, Amh.Gaf. läbbäsa, Arg. läbbäsa, Har. läbäsa.

meaning: (T) 'body of troops, princes'; the root is perhaps lbs.

origin_explanation: from Gk. lebēs 'caldron', with the Geez plural marker -ät. See also balbisät.

meaning: overlay (with metal, wood), plate, gild, smear (with a coating)'; 'albata, causative; talabja, pass. of labata; also 'welter (in one's blood)'; labati (Gr.35), act. part. of labata; labut (fem. labatt, for labatt) 'overlaid, plated, smeared with a coating'; labtat 'act of overlaying, plating'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: perhaps related to Ar. laṭa (lwt) 'plaster, overlay with clay', Heb. laṭ (lwt) 'enwrap, envelop' (so also Armbruster 59, Leslau 1958:28), with alter-nance b: w. ETHIOPIC: Te. läbbäṭa 'besmear, cover', Tna.Amh. läbbäṭä 'overlay (with metal, wood, etc)'. See also labada.

meaning: short, sleeveless tunic worn by monks'
origin_explanation: from Gk. lebētōnarion, same; also Amh. labiṭonar. när. See also labiṭonär.
from Gk. lebētōnarion, same; also Amh. labiṭonar. See labiṭon.

meaning: 'possess understanding, under-stand, comprehend, perceive, be clever, be mindful, devise, take heed, beware'; 'alabbawa, caus. of labbabwa; also 'instruct, give understanding, make intelligent (Gr.33) inspire'; talabbawa 'be comprehended, be understood'; labbawō 'intelligence, cleverness, prudence'; labbawi, see labbaw; also 'rational, intelligent, who (that) reasons'; labbaw 'wise, understanding, intelligent, comprehending, prudent, skilled, learned'; (labbäwa 'skillfully'); labbawē 'intelligence, prudence, mind, intel-lect'; labbunā, labbonā 'understanding, comprehen-sion, intelligence, insight, wisdom, reason, rea-soning, prudence, attention, skill, cleverness'; (ba-labbunā 'attentively'); labbunawi 'wise, understanding, attentive'.
'intelligence, wisdom, pru-dence'; 'alabbawi, part. of 'alabbawa; malabbaw 'teacher'; also participle of 'alab-bawa;
origin_explanation: denominative either directly from labb 'heart' or from a form such as labbawi 'rational'. The denomi-natives formed with final -wa are frequent forma-tions in Geez; cp. gassawa, from gass. For labb, see labbabwa, above. For other denominatives from labb, cp. Tna.Amh. läbbāmā 'be intelligent, be prudent, be perspicacious', Gur. läbbāmā 'economize, be thrifty', from labbam 'intelligent, careful', from labb. Amh. läbbäwā 'be intelligent' is taken from Geez. See also labbabwa.

meaning: 'err, be ignorant, blunder, be undecided, be perplexed, offend'; 'albaya 'induce into error, mislead, (T) make disappear'; 'alabbaya (K*), caus. of labbaya; 'astalabbaya, (Gr.492) 'astaläbäya 'consider something as arisen through error and reject it, make disappear, consider to be in error, be reckless, (K*) mislead'; labbuy 'erroneous, arisen through error, reck-less, blamed because of having been in error, (T) lazy';
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Reinisch 1887:250 compares Bil.Qua. läb, Kham. lib 'fall, attack'.

meaning: 'first month of the year (maska-ram 'September'), beginning of the dry season'.

'write, roll around, cover'; 'alfafa (T), causative; also 'decree'; lafāf 'scroll, (T) writing'; lafāfā ṣədəq 'Bandlet of Righteousness, Decree of Righteousness', a scroll containing magic names which is buried with the dead in order to enable their entry into heaven; see Euringer, Orientalia 9 (1940).76-99; Löfgren, OS 11 (1962).95-120; E.A. Wallis Budge, The Bandlet of Righteousness [London, 1929], Walker 21, 53);
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. laffa 'wrap up, roll up', PbH. lāqaq 'wrap', Aram. lapap, Syr. lap 'join', Md. lpp, Akk. lapāpu 'wrap'. ETHIOPIC: Te. läffäfa 'roll up, decree', Tna.Amh. läffäfa.

meaning: 'adhere, reach by pursu-ing (T.Y.M) arrive, be near, receive';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. lafiqa 'obtain' (that is, 'reach thing'). ETHIOPIC: Te. (ta)läfäqa 'be pressed together (that is, 'stick to one another'), Gur. (tä)läfäqä 'be pressed (people in a room), be crammed'.

meaning: 'be stained, be dirty, be smeared with dirt'.

origin_explanation: SEMITIC: perhaps Ar. lafaqa 'embellish (a na-with lies), forge (a story)'.

meaning: 'knead, crush'; talfaṣa (K*), passive; lafus 'kneaded, mixed'; lafṣat 'kneading' (n.);
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Tna. läfäṣä 'plaster', Amh. läfäṭä 'knead'. See also laft.
perhaps derived from lafaya 'divide, share'.
related to lafaṣa 'knead' (see above), or would it be a graphic confusion with qafṭ?

meaning: 'separate, divide, move here and there, (Y) err'; talafaya, passive;
origin_explanation: denominative from lafe (see fe, above). See also laft.

meaning: 'the milk secreted a few days before and after childbed';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Sh. lega 'suck (teats)'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. lägä 'colostrum', Amh. laga (from Geez).

meaning: 'sew, patch, trim the edge of the leather, be superficially healed'; note that Amh. läggämä (translation of G. lagaba given by Y 324) also means 'be inactive, be lazy, shrink', meanings that come close to 'be superficially healed';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: perhaps Ar. lagama 'sew', with alternance of labials (Ambros). ETHIOPIC: Te. lägbä 'patch', Tna.Amh. läggäbä; a variant of läqqäbä.

SEMITIC: Ar. (Yemen) lagāgā 'drool, drivel', luğāğ 'saliva, drivel' (W. Müller). ETHIOPIC: Amh. läggägä; passed into Cushitic: Qem. lagāg 'saliva'. Conti Rossini 1912:223 com-pares Qua. läh, Bil. laq.
ETHIOPIC: Tna.Amh. läggämä 'be inactive', Te. lägmā 'be incurable', Gur. läggämä 'work slackly'.

meaning: 'rein (v.), bridle, curb, restrain, hold in check, shut off' (denomi-native from lag'ām); 'alg'ama, causative; also 'rein, bridle, to si-lence'; talag'ama, passive; also 'be silent, be quiet'; lag'āmi, act. part. of lag'ama; also 'groom'; lag'ām (Gr) 'bridled, restrained, repressed, over-come'; lag'ām (pl. lag'āmāt, 'alg'ām) 'bridle, rein, halter, band, tie'; malg'ām (Gr) 'bridle'; malg'ām, malg'āmi (Lt) 'which curbs, which binds';
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Noeldeke 1910:41 derives G. lag'ām from a rarely attested Aram.Syr. lagāmā, lugmā 'bridle', but it is perhaps rather borrowed from Ar. ligām 'bridle', for which see Fraenkel 100; also in Te. lagām 'bridle', Tna.Amh.Arg.Gaf.Gur. lag'ām. Brockelmann 1928:359 connects the root with Syr. lugmā 'jaw'.

meaning: 'cake, wafer'; (note that Amh. qäfo 'beehive', a transla-

origin_explanation: From Gk. legeōn, from Lat. legio 'body of soldiers'
From Gk. logeion 'oracle'; it translates Heb. ḥōšen
from Gk. laganon 'thin and broad cake'; also in Syr. legnā, lagnā 'a cake made of flour and oil'.

meaning: 'kind of measure, utensil serving as a measure, container for grain, (T) pot'; also = Amh. maṃma° 'shelter erected on poles or in a tree for guarding crops or cattle'
origin_explanation: from Gk. lagēnos 'measure for liquids'; also in Amh. lagān 'pottery vessel'; in Semitic: Ar. lugna (for which see Fraenkel 130-1), Aram.-Syr. lagīnā 'bottle, vessel', Md. lugiana; Akkadian liginnu, li-gittu quoted by Drower-Macuch 232 in the meaning of 'measure of grain' (on the authority of Zimmern) is registered with this meaning neither in von Soden nor in CAD. See also lagnat. ETHIOPIC: Reinisch 1890:27 compares Sa. algēn 'case, box'.
lagnata (M) 'prepare porridge' (denominative); for 'container for grain, beehive', see lag°an, above.
from Gk. ligyros; same; see also lagiryōn.
from Gk. ligyrion, diminutive of ligyros; same; also in Syr. ligwrayyūn. See also ligir.
could be derived from walaqa, below, but the connection is doubtful.

meaning: 'abyss, depth, pool of water, whirlpool, (D 1427) a piece of land' (in the expression lag°ata madr);
origin_explanation: lag°ata (K*) 'be deep' (denominative); SEMITIC: Ar. lagg, lugga 'depth of the sea, abyss, gulf'. ETHIOPIC: Te. laggāt 'depth, whirlpool', Tna. lag°āt, nāg°āt.

lahaba 'burn (tr.), ignite'; talahaba 'be inflamed, be ignited'; lāḥub 'ignited, inflamed, warm'; lāḥb 'flame, heat, glow, warmth, per-spiration'; lāḥbat (Lt) 'heat, (Gr) flame'; alḥābi (J) 'who ignites'; SEMITIC: Ar. lahiba 'flame' (v.), Mh. (n.), Heb. lahab, lahbā, Syr. lhb in šalhēb 'flame' (v.), Aram. lahābā 'flame' (n.), šalḥēb 'inflame' (v.), labu 'contagious fever'. For the second formation šlhb in the various Semitic languages, see Rabin, EI 9 (1969), 152. ETHIOPIC: Amh. labā 'be warm, perspire', lahab 'flame' (n.), from Geez; Te. lāhaba 'perspire', Tna. lahbāt 'perspiration' (for European root for 'sweat' going back to Buck 264). The root lhb > lb, reduplicated as lblb, occurs in Te. lāḥlāba 'singe, kindle', lāḥlāb 'flame', lāḥlāba 'flame, flare', Amh. lāḥlābā, lāḥlāb 'flame' (n.), Gur. lāḥlāballā 'burn the surface'.
probably to be identified with lahaha 'be humid'. For a connection between 'draw water' and 'be humid', cp. Amh. qādda 'draw water' and qāddaw 'it rained heavily'.

meaning: 'iron ring of the plow', (T*) = Amh. alālo 'round, black stone used for crushing cotton seeds';
origin_explanation: lāḥlawa (T.M) 'make the iron ring of the plow' (denominative).
one would expect lāhlaya; to be connected with lahaya, below.
variant of lahaba, above.

(lāḥma gadām (Lt) 'buffalo'); (wāwā lāḥm 'calf'); SEMITIC: Hommel 1879:103, n.2 compares it with Heb. lhm in mitlaḥamīm 'who are swallowed greedy', Ar. laḥīma 'swallow greedily', lāḥm 'aged ox'. The identification of Ethiopic lḥm with Heb. leḥem 'bread' (that is, 'food' in general, 'bread' in Hebrew, 'meat' in Arabic) which goes back to Isenberg 6 and is taken up by Armbruster 29, and Ullendorff, VT 6 (1956), 192 is doubtful. G. Krotkoff, WZKM (1969), 76-82 likewise expresses doubts concerning this comparison. ETHIOPIC: Tna. laḥmi, laḥmi, lam°cow', Amh.A. lam, Har. lam, Gaf. alām'. There is perhaps a connection with Cushitic: Bil. lōwi 'cow', Kham lōwa, with m > w.

(za-yalaḥaq 'elder'); yalḥaqa, alḥaqa 'bring up, rear, make grow, make grow old'; talaḥaqa 'become old, become a presbyter' (nominative of laḥiq); talaḥaqa (K*) 'compete with someone in height, status, and so on'; °astalāḥaqa (K*), caus. of talāḥaqa; laḥiq (fem. laḥiqt, laḥaqt) 'grown-up, adult, old man, senior, presbyter';
laḥqāt 'old age, mature age'; laḥqān 'seniority, (TS) old age'; laḥqannā 'old age, dignity of elder, principality';

malḥaqt (pl. malḥaqtāt, malāḥaqt, malāḥaqt) (T) malḥaqt 'eldest, elder, senior, chief'; malḥaqa bet (Lt) 'steward'; SEMITIC: Soq. dileq 'be numerous' coming from (who, which) 'ileq 'is numerous', pl. yileq, from ileq (il being the plural of the relative pronoun) from lḥq (Leslau 1938:129), Heb. laḥāqā 'seniors, elders' suggested by Ludolf 635 (so also Driver, JTS 29 [1928],394, Ullendorff, VT 6 [1956],194 and Barr

meaning: Aḥlā 'vanish, disappear, depart from, (T) be sent away, be dispersed';
origin_explanation: talāḥsa (D 1425), talahasa (T) 'glide away, es-cape, vanish, disappear, (M) elapse, (T) be sent away'; ETHIOPIC: connected with Cushitic: Som. lāsa 'bring to an end'.

lāḥaya 'comfort, play'; (T.Y.M) lāḥaya 'comfort one another' is undoubtedly for talāḥaya; alḥaya 'comfort, free someone from anxiety, (T) entertain, amuse'; talahaya (T.Gr.20) 'be in motion, be in move-ment, be agitated'; talāḥaya, talahaya, (T) talahaya 'play, amuse oneself, enjoy oneself, amuse one another, comfort one another, play a musical instrument, (Gr.492) play together, (T) go here and there'; °astalāḥaya 'amuse, entertain, divert'; talāḥāyi 'who plays, player'; talāḥāyā (K) 'movement'; talāḥo 'play, amusement, mirth, pastimes'; talḥīt 'play, amusement, entertainment'; malḥāy (Gr.20) 'place reserved for playing'; mastalaḥāy (K*), act. part. of °astalāḥaya; SEMITIC: Ar. laḥā (lḥw, lḥy) 'amuse oneself, play', Heb. lḥh in mitlaḥah 'play', (Pr. 26:18-19), recorded in the existing dictionaries under lḥh, is interpreted as 'trifle, enjoy oneself' and connected with the root lḥy by Kopf 152.

meaning: (T.MA) lahda ለሀድ, (L) lahda ለሀድ 'be tender, be gentle, be delicate, be pampered'.
(fem. lahadd) 'tender, delicate, luxurious, pampered, given to pleasure, (T) old woman' (< 'delicate');

origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Amh. lādallādā 'be soft' (reduplicated *lhd > ld); also in Cushitic: Som. lid 'weakness, frailty'. See also lahda.

meaning: (M) 'be fixed, be established'; lohd (Gr.20) 'arranged with stones, bridge';
origin_explanation: MA quotes lahda = Amh. čārrāsā 'finish'; it should be rather tāčārrāsā 'be finished'.

meaning: (KBT 5) 'fringed rug or carpet made from silk or from other thread';
origin_explanation: from Ar. lihāf; also Amh. lāhaf.

meaning: (T.Y) talahafa 'be troubled, worry, have concern for, be annoyed, be importuned, be affected by a matter, (T) be acquainted with one another (originally, 'have concern for one another');
'be troubled, worry, have concern for, be annoyed, be importuned, be affected by a matter, (T) be acquainted with one another (originally, 'have concern for one another');

origin_explanation: SEMITIC: related to Ar. lahifa 'grieve for', lahf 'sorrow'.

meaning: lahha, lahaha 'be humid, (Y) be soft, be smooth';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. lahia 'stick together (eyelids)' where the effect of moisture is involved (Ambros), ('a)lahha 'give a continuous rain (cloud)', Heb. lah 'moist, fresh', PBh. lihle'h 'moisten', Aramaic. lahlaḥ, Pal.Syr. lh 'moisture'.

meaning: 'moisten, cool off, soften', (KG) = Amh. abbo abbo alā 'ask St. Gābrā mānfas qaddus for help'; (T) also = Amh. abāzza 'multiply' is probably a wrong interpretation.
'fashion, form, mold, create, shape, (Gr.22) make earthenware';
(fem. of luhuk) 'formed, fashioned, form, formation, creation, work, handiwork, earthenware, (T) clay, (Lt) vase';

origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: from Cushitic: Bed. luk 'clay'; Praetorius, ZDMG 47 (1893), 390 following Reinisch 1884:89 derives it from Cushitic: Kham. roq°a 'clay', Af. rugā, Bil. darawqā. The derivation of Ethiopic śākla 'clay' from ś + kl from G. lhk 'mold, shape' suggested by Praetorius 1879:94 is unlikely.

meaning: (T) lāhlāha ለሀለሀ, (Y) lāhlāha ለሀለሀ (instead of ለሀለሀ) 'be humid, be damp';

origin_explanation: seems to be a wrong reading for lāhləht (see Dillmann 30, under lahluh); as for the meaning 'soft', see lāhlāha. M.19 renders it 'pearl'; he apparently misread Tayyā's Amh. lal 'soft' (translation of G. lalt) for lul 'pearl'.

meaning: (K*) lahama ለሀመ (T.Y) 'be strong, (T.Y.KG) become severe or worse (referring to a disease)';

origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. lahama 'consolidate, solder, mend'. G. lāhama is perhaps borrowed from Ar. lahama rather than being a cognate. ETHIOPIC: Te. lāhama 'hold together by means of glue'.

meaning: (D.Gr.21), (T.Y) 'be tender, be soft, be reduced to powder, be pulverized, be flexible, be supple, be feeble, be infirm, be moist';
'tender, soft, supple, weakened, feeble, (Gr.21) reduced to powder, pulverized';
malhami (read thus instead of ahlāmi, malhami of Gr. 21), act. part. of alhama; malhām 'place where one reduces to powder' (instead of mahlām of Gr.21).
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: related to Ar. lahama 'languidness' (so also Salu 1). ETHIOPIC: Tna. lāhamā 'be pulverized', lāhamā 'be soft, be tender', Amh. lamā 'be pulverized, be soft', Arg. lāhim 'soft', Har. lēhama 'become soft', Gaf. lāmāmā 'soft', Gur. lāmā 'be soft, be smooth' (Selti, Wolane also lāmālāmā). Note that Gaf. and Gur. can go back to lamlama, below.
SEMITIC: Ar. lahiqa 'cling to'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. malhəq 'anchor', Amh. malhaq.

meaning: 'lick, be parched (with thirst) and thrust out the tongue to lick'; (Y) lahasa = Amh. amarā 'be pretty' is strange; possibly a misprint for lahaya;
(Lt), talāhasa (M) 'move when hissing (tongue of a snake), move (scale, by counterbalance)';

origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. lahisa 'lick', Mh. lahās, Akk. lāsu 'taste, lick'. ETHIOPIC: Te. lāhasa 'lick', Tna. lāhasā, Amh.Gur. lasā, Arg. lāhasa, Har. lāhasa; developed into the meaning 'tongue' in Tna. malhas, Amh. mǝlas, Gaf. mǝlasā. The root passed into Cushitic: Bed. lehas. See also lassān, below.

meaning: (K*) lah˚asā ለሀሰ 'murmur' only in Jos.10:21, one of the three passages quoted by Dillmann, has lahasa the meaning of 'murmur' (according to the Septuagint), but 'whet the tongue' against (according to the Hebrew text). Likewise in Ex.11:7, the Hebrew text has 'whet (the tongue)' while the Greek text has 'snarl'. Also, the meaning 'murmur' cannot easily be rendered by lahasa with object suffix pronouns as is the case in Ex. 11:7 and Jos. 10:21. A meaning close to 'lick (them) to do harm' is perhaps more appropriate for lahasa in these passages than 'murmur'. The same meaning can be applied to Judith 11:19;

origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: for 'murmur', cp. Te. lāhasā 'pant', and see below lahosasa.

meaning: (T.Y), lahsa 'be chased away, be sent away, be dispersed';
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Te. (tā)lāhasa 'be swept away'.

meaning: (Gr.23) ləhosās 'whisper, soft whispering, style of singing';

origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Heb. lāhas 'whisper' (v), Aram. lahāš, Syr. lāheš, Md. lihšā 'whispering' (also nhš 'whisper'), Ug. lhš, Akk. lahāšu 'whisper' (v.). ETHIOPIC: Tna. lohšāšā, lāhošāšā 'whisper', Amh. lahosās 'whispering' (from Geez). See also lahasa II.

meaning: (TS) lahəsa (T) lahəsa (Gr.24) 'cause to peel', talhəsa, passive; lahəsi (Gr.24) 'who peels'; lahəs (Gr.24) 'peeled'; lahəs (T) lahəs 'bark, rind'; lahəsat (Gr.25) 'peeling' (n.);
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: related to Ar. lahəsa 'extract the pith of' perhaps also Hebrew hālas 'draw off' (so also Dillmann 34), Aram. hālas 'take off, undress', Syr. halles 'take spoils'. ETHIOPIC: Te. lahəsa 'bark (trees)', Tna. lahəsā, Amh.Gur. laṭa, Arg. lāhaṭa, Har. lēhaṭa, Gur. leṭaṭa, from leṭa with a reduplicated last radical; passed into Cushitic; Bil. leheṣa 'bark (a tree)'.

meaning: (T) talahəṭa (K.Gr.24) talahəṭa 'be resplendent, adorn oneself, attire oneself'; talahəṭaya (Lt) 'adorn oneself for others'; lahəṭuy 'pretty, handsome, brilliant'; lahəṭuy 'beautiful'; lahəṭuy (fem. lahəṭuy, lāhiṭ) 'beautiful, handsome, beauty, splendor, brightness'; lahəṭuyat (Lt) 'beautiful things'; lahəṭuyannā (Gr.24) 'beauty'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Barth 1893:3 connects the root hly with (with metathesis): Ar. halā (hly) 'adorn, adornments' (going back to hly); so also Reinisch 1895:157. The connection with Ar. lahā (hly) 'peel off', Syr. lahā 'rub off' suggested by Brockelmann 1928:363 is unlikely.

meaning: (T) lahawa (T) see lāhawa. lahawa 'mourn for, lament, bewail, weep, grieve, groan, sigh'; 'alhawa, (Gr.23) 'alāhawa, causative; 'astalāhawa (Gr.23) 'make weep often, weep often'; lahāwi (fem. lahāwit) 'mourner'; lahāw, lahāw (fem. lahāw) 'who mourns, grieving'; lāh, (T) lāh 'mourning, grief, dirge, (Gr.23) tears'; lāhəw (Lt) 'mourning, grief'; mastalāhāwi (Gr.23) 'who makes weep'; lāhəw (T) lāhəw 'prattle', Tna lah bela 'pester, annoy'.
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Te. lāhəw (lhw) 'prattle', Tna lah bela 'pester, annoy'.

meaning: (T) 'scarlet thread' could it be connected with lahaya 'be pretty'? See also lay.

(Gr.24) lahaya (T) lahaya 'be pretty, be beautiful, be shiny, have a sheen (skin)'; (H 224 wrongly renders Amh. wāzza [translation of G. lahaya] by 'perspire'; it also means 'be pretty'); 'alhaya (Gr.24) causative;

'pave, level off, make easy'; talahəda (Gr), passive; lahādi (Gr), act. part. of lahəda; lahəd (Gr), pass. part. of lahəda; lahədat (Gr) 'act of paving'; the meaning 'make easy' is to be identified with lahəda (and, in fact, lahəda could be another spelling for lahəda); as for 'pave, level off', it can likewise be derived from the basic meaning 'be gentle, be easy' > 'be paved, leveled off'.

(Y) lāhlaha (should read lāhlaha) 'become weak, become slack, be soft, be loose, languish'; lāhlaha (K*), causative; lāhlah 'soft, slack, loose, languid'; lāhlah 'weakness, softness, slackness'; lāhlahat (Gr.39) 'softness'; lāhlahənnā, see lāhlahə.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: related to Ar. lahla'a 'be soft, be lax' reduced to lh in the reduplicated form lhlh (see lamla'a for a similar development). ETHIOPIC: Te. lāh bela 'relent', lāhlaha 'be soft', lāhlaha (from 'alāhlaha) 'render soft', Tna. lahlahe 'be loose', Arg. lāhlāha, Har. lahla baya, Amh.Gur. lalla, Amh. lahlu 'soft' (from Geez); passed into Cushitic; Bil. lāh y.

meaning: (T.M), malāhaq (T reprint) 'kind of basket for food'; a plural of a singular *malhaq, malhaq.

origin_explanation: From Ar. lakk, of Middle-Indic lakkha origin.

meaning: (D) lakā (Gr.38) 'inscribe, impress, imprint, mark, set down in writing, compose, (J) coin (money)'; 'alkāwa, causative; talakāwa, passive; lakā'i, act. part. of lakā; lakāw, pass. part. of lakā; lakāw (Lt) 'text, original' (n); malkā 'act of inscribing, impressing, recording' malkā 'form, image, figure, effigy, icon, portrait, likeness, aspect, a seal impression of poetry (consisting of five verses) which eulogizes a saint by enumerating and praising the various parts of the body'; malkā, malkā (KG) 'means of writing'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: SAr. (Qataban) lkw ' (pl) (schriftlich) gelegt' (Müller). ETHIOPIC: Tna. lākkā'e 'measure', Amh.Gur. lākka, Arg. ləkka. For G. malkā, cp. Tna. mālka 'appearance', Amh.Arg.Gur. mālka. The root passed into Cushitic: Dar. likkē-su 'measure', Al. milk 'appearance'. See also lakā II.

meaning: (D) lakā (T) 'affix, nail down, fasten to, adhere to'; talakā'a (K) 'adhere to one another, be fastened to one another'; 'astalakā'a 'make adhere to one another (Gr.492) make something adhere to oneself'; lakāw 'affixed, fastened to'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. lakī'a 'stick to'. ETHIOPIC: Te. lāk'a 'fasten with nails', Tna. 'hit, strike'. It is possible to connect the meanings of lakā II with those of lakā I.

meaning: (D 1393. T.M) 'kind of fruit or vegetable' (Amh. wāzā);
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Amh. lakākəh 'vine that has an edible root'.

meaning: (T.M) 'write'; probably a misprint for lakā'a, for which see above.

(Y.M) 'hit someone's chest, hit'; 'alk'ama 'dash against, thrust, strike, throw down, stamp the ground, hit'; note that the Amharic translation ṣafā 'write' given by Tayyā is wrongly copied from the line above; lak'mat (Lt) 'act of hitting'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. lakama 'punch, strike with the fist'. ETHIOPIC: Amh. lākkāmā, lākk'amā 'punch, strike with the fist'.
from Gk. lekanē 'dish, pot, pan'; see also lak'ant.

meaning: (T) 'pan, vase, pot'; variant of lak'ant, above.

(T) 'become dull (eyes), become dim, be weak (eyes), be feeble, (Y.M) not understand, be ignorant'; 'alkawa (K.T) 'make dull, make dim, weaken'; lakəw, fem. lakut (Gr.37) 'weakened (eyesight), blinded, who does not see clearly'; lakəwat (Gr.38) 'weakening (of eyes)'; 'alkəw, malkəw (Gr.37) 'that weakens the eyesight';
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: related to Te. lakka (lkk) 'be stupid'.
variant of lak'aya; see below.

meaning: (T) 'touch, smear, be- smear (K.Y) reach'; 'alkafa 'cause to touch'; talakafa, passive; talakafa 'touch one another, (Gr.492) one near one another'; 'astalakafa (Gr.39) 'dash against each other', lakf, pass. part. of lakafa; lakfat 'contact, touch';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: perhaps related to Ar. laqafa 'catch'.

meaning: (D 1426) 'touch (with the tongue or the finger)'; The connection with Syr. laḥak 'lay hold of, seize' suggested by Barth 1893:27 is unlikely. ETHIOPIC: Tna. lākāfā 'touch (with the tongue or the finger)', Amh. lākkāfā 'touch with the nose to sniff'. The connection with Te. lāk'a 'throw' suggested by Littmann-Höfner 43 is unlikely.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: The connection with Syr. laḥak 'lay hold of, seize' suggested by Barth 1893:27 is unlikely. ETHIOPIC: Tna. lākāfā 'touch (with the tongue or the finger)', Amh. lākkāfā 'touch with the nose to sniff'. The connection with Te. lāk'a 'throw' suggested by Littmann-Höfner 43 is unlikely.
SEMITIC: Ar. lakaya 'touch lightly, anoint'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. läkāyä 'touch lightly, anoint'.

meaning: 'quarrel with one another, dispute, discuss, wrangle, debate, contend, strive, revile, poke, reprimand, blame, (Y) importune, deride'; lāk"a 'altercation, strife, conflict'
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Reinsich 1887:252 connects it with Bil. lag 'accuse, reprimand'. Fleisch 239, n. 2 thinks that the verb is a denominative.
ETHIOPIC: Tna.Amh. lila, lilo, Te. lilo; also in Cushitic: Bil. lilo, Bed. lalē. See also lolo, tulo.
see lilo.
from Ar. lu'lu'; also Te.Tna.Amh.Gur. lul, Har. lu'lu'.
is there any connection with lilo, lolo 'vulture'?

meaning: 'night'; (näfqa lelit manfaqa lelit 'midnight'); 'alälayä 'spend the night (doing something), do something early in the night, rise early in the night' (denominative);
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. layl 'night', Sar. (Qataban) lyl, Soq. lihe, Heb. layil, Aram. lelā, lelēyā, Syr. lelyā, Md. lilia, Ug. ll, Akk. lilātu, lilātu. ETHIOPIC: Te. lali, Tna. läyti, Amh. let, lelit.
misprint for läbbäwi, läbbäwi.

meaning: 'inflame, light up, kindle, make sparkle, make scintillate, illuminate, singe, scorch'; 'alolawa (Lt), causative;
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: related to Ar. la'la'a 'shine, be bright' (Dillmann 53; so also Fleisch 217; note that his translation 'remuer la langue' refers to lawlawa, occasionally written lolawa). ETHIOPIC: Tna. lälašäwā 'singe, scorch'.

meaning: 'block up a hole, bank a fire';
origin_explanation: seems to be opposite of lolawa I.
secondary form of lelayä.

meaning: 'separate, disjoin, divide, distinguish, make a difference (between), segregate'; 'alelayä, causative; talelayä, passive, reflexive; lelāyi (J), act. part. of lelayä; liluy (fem. liläyt, lilīt) 'separated, distinguished, segregated'; liläy 'separation, distinction'; malelīt (M) 'member of the body, joint'; talelayä (Lt) 'separation'; malelīt (pl. maläylay, maläyläy) 'joint, limb, member of the body, sense, (T.KG) genital organs';
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: G. lelayä is a reduplicated root ly; so also Te. ('a)lelā 'separate, distinguish', Tna. läläyä. The root ly(y) occurs in Amh.Gur. läyä 'distinguish', Arg. läyya, Har. läya, Gaf. liggä (for which see Leslau 1956:9). A reduplicated root also occurs in Amh.Arg.Gur. lela 'another, different'. For malelīt 'joint, limb' coming from a root 'separate, disjoin', cp. Ar. mafsil 'joint, articulation', from fasala 'separate, disjoin'. The root also occurs in Cushitic: Sa. layä 'part, division'. See also ma-layä.
transcription of Heb.Aram. lämā.
could it be a misprint for lop?
from Ar. lūmī; also in Te.Tna. lomin, Tna. also lämin, Amh.Gur. lomi; in Cushitic: Af. limi, lōmi, Som. liin, Sa. limin, Bed. lemūn. See Müller, ZDMG (1981).400-1.

meaning: 'be resplendent, scintillate, shine'; 'alam'a (Lt), causative; talam'a 'be resplendent, gleam, scintillate'; lam'u (K*) 'shining, resplendent';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. lama'a 'shine, gleam'. ETHIOPIC: Te. läm'a 'blossom' (perhaps to be identified with läm'a 'be green, be tender'; see lamlama) with metathesis in lä'ama 'gain profit'; Tna. läm'e 'prosper, flourish'; with metathesis in lä'amä 'profit'. Amh. lämma 'prosper, be fertile (land,). See also lamha.

meaning: 'be accustomed to, be familiar with, be used to, be trained, learn'; 'almada 'accustom, be accustomed to, be wont to, (Lt) tame'; talamada (K*) 'be a habit'; talamada 'be accustomed to, have the habit, be accustomed to one another'; lämäd 'custom, habit, usage'; (za-lämäd 'habitual, customary'); lämud (fem. lämädd < lämädīt) 'who accustoms himself, accustomed, expert, usual, tamed, trained, (Lt) addicted'; lämädāwi (Gr.26) 'habitual, customary'; 'almadi, malmadi (Gr.26) 'who accustoms', talmid, see below.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Heb. lämad 'learn', limmūd 'accustomed', Syr. lammed 'accustom', Ug. lmd 'learn', Akk. lamādu 'comprehend, learn'. ETHIOPIC: Te. lämda 'get accustomed, learn', Tna. lämda, Amh. lämmädä, Gur. nämädä (for lämädä) 'love'.
ETHIOPIC: Amh. lämd.

meaning: 'wooden shovel used for winnowing trampled grain';
origin_explanation: from Amh. lameda; also Gur. lameda; perhaps from Cushitic: Had. kam. lamēda. See also läydä.
see lamva.

meaning: 'be verdant, bud, become soft, be smooth, be mild, become tender, be flexible, be fresh'; 'alamlama, causative; lämlum 'soft, tender, mild, smooth, flexible, delicate, fresh, verdant'; lamlam, same; lämlämē 'verdure, freshness, moisture, softness, tenderness, delicacy'; malamlām, part. of 'alamlama;
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Rūžička 102 connects Ethiopic lmlm with PBH. lblb 'germinate, flourish, sprout, be green', with alternance of labials. Armbruster 27 compares Ar. lama'a 'gleam'. ETHIOPIC: the reduplicated root goes back to lm? (= laryngeal which became zero) in Te. lämläma 'become green, sprout', Tna. lämlämä, Amh. lämlämä 'become verdant, send out shoots', Gaf. lämläm 'a 'tender'. The non-reduplicated lm' occurs in Te. läm'a 'be green, be tender', Tna. läm'e, Amh. lämma, Gur. lämā, Gaf. lämä. The root also occurs in Cushitic: Qaf. läläm (from lmlm) 'be verdant, be fresh', Kham. lilem. See also lamva, lam'a, lamha.

meaning: = Amh. näqqašä 'shape wood or stone, tattoo'.

origin_explanation: from Amh. lämmänä 'beg', the root also occurs in Tna. lämmänä 'beg', Te. lämmano 'begging', Gur. lämmän 'beggar'.
lit. 'for what?'; see la, män.
seems to be composed of la- and mänzuš 'accustomed to pleasure'; see also manzašä, below.

meaning: lampapa (MA) 'shine, be bright' (denominative); from Amh. lampa 'lamp' (a variant of lamba, from Ar. lambā, of Italian origin); also in Te.Tna.Gur. lampa, lamba, Har. lāmba. See also lap, lanpās.
origin_explanation: from Amh. lampa 'lamp' (a variant of lamba, from Ar. lambā, of Italian origin); also in Te.Tna.Gur. lampa, lamba, Har. lāmba.
From Gk. lampsanē 'charlock (herb)'.

phonetics: lamaṣa (D*, Gr.26) lamda 'be smooth, be soft, be polished, be plain, be bare, be bald';
meaning: 'be smooth, be soft, be polished, be plain, be bare, be bald'; lamaṣṣa, causative; also 'make slippery'; 'almaṣa, causative; lamāṣi (Gr.26), act. part. of lamaṣa; lamuṣ 'smooth, slippery, soft, polished, bald, (D*) hard, rough' (but in the sentence quoted by Ludolf it could also mean 'slippery'); lamuṣ 'leper, leprous'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. maliṣ 'smooth', with metathesis (Dillmann 36). Heb. mls (ni'al) 'be smooth'. ETHIOPIC: Te. lämäṣa 'be smooth, be moist', Tna. lämäṣa 'become soft', Amh. lamuṭ 'smooth, polished'. See also lamṣ.

meaning: 'leprosy, scab'; lamaṣa (T) 'become a leper' (probably denominative); (za-lamṣ, pl. ǝlla lamṣ 'leper', lit. 'of [who has, have] leprosy'); lamuṣ 'leper, leprous'; lamṣāwi 'leper, leprous'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. lamaz 'white spots on a horse's lower lip' (Armbruster 35). ETHIOPIC: Tna. lämṣi 'leprosy', Amh.Arg.Gur. lämṭ; passed into Cushitic: Al.Tem. lämṭa. The noun 'leper' is perhaps to be derived from lamaṣa in the meaning 'be bare, be bald', since the part of the body that is affected by leprosy is white and gives the impression of being bald.

phonetics: lanpāne , (J) lanpāne , (T) lanpane 'stretcher, throne, chariot, (T) headrest in the form of a half-moon, (J) tent, small tent within a bigger tent';
meaning: 'stretcher, throne, chariot, (T) headrest in the form of a half-moon, (J) tent, small tent within a bigger tent';
origin_explanation: from Gk. lampēnē 'covered chariot'; also in Te. lanpāne 'litter, tent', Amh. lämpāne, lämpāne 'throne for the monarch or prince'. See also lap, lanpās.
(Li) lanpasa (T) 'shine, scintillate, sparkle' (denominative); from Gk. lampas 'lamp'. See also lampā.

phonetics: lanpasa (Y.MA) 'wear a garment, wrap oneself in a garment';
meaning: 'wear a garment, wrap oneself in a garment';

origin_explanation: misprint for lanṣewon .

phonetics: lanqā () 'sacerdotal vestment, formerly used by the ecclesiastics and the nobility';
meaning: 'sacerdotal vestment, formerly used by the ecclesiastics and the nobility';
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Amh. lanqa; see Hammerschmidt 1970: 154.

phonetics: lanṣ , länṣ 'bandage, linen, (J) shroud, belt of monks, (Sw. in D 47) priest's turban';
meaning: 'bandage, linen, (J) shroud, belt of monks, (Sw. in D 47) priest's turban';
origin_explanation: possibly Gk. lention 'cloth, towel, apron worn by monks' (Ludolf 477). See also lanṣewon.

phonetics: lanṣewon (D.K) 'bandage, towel, linen'; see lanṣ, lanpewon.
lpp, lappa (MA) = Amh. lämälläma which, without a context, can mean 'become verdant, prosper, prune (a tree)';
meaning: = Amh. lämälläma which, without a context, can mean 'become verdant, prosper, prune (a tree)';

phonetics: lapā (T) 'grass', (MA) = Amh. zär HC is undoubtedly a misprint for sar "IC 'grass'.
meaning: 'grass', (MA) = Amh. zär HC is undoubtedly a misprint for sar "IC 'grass'.

phonetics: liq (pl. liqān, liqānāt, liqāwǝnt) 'chief, senior, old man, aged, elder, master, magistrate, learned man, presbyter';
meaning: 'chief, senior, old man, aged, elder, master, magistrate, learned man, presbyter'; liqa kahnāt 'archpriest, high priest'; liqa pappāsāt 'patriarch, archbishop'; liqa malā'ǝkt 'archangel'; liqa nafs (Gr.19) 'spiritual leader'; liqa hamar 'captain of a ship'; liqa harā 'chief of the army'; (sab'a liqānāt 'Septuagint', lit. 'seventy learned men'); liqännā 'seniority'.
origin_explanation: derived from lahqa, above. See also loqā.
perhaps from Cushitic: Bil. laqā'o 'silver'.
ETHIOPIC: related to Te. lägda 'play, jest', läggädä 'make fun'; passed into Cushitic: Sa. Qem. lägät.

phonetics: laqqada II (T) 'exhibit to view, aspire to'.

meaning: 'lend' (zayaleqqah 'lender'); 'alaqqaha 'cause to lend'; talaqqaha 'borrow'; (zayaleqqah 'borrower'); laqqāhi 'creditor, lender'; laqqāhi (K*) 'lent'; laqqāh 'loan, giving a loan'; (sab'a laqqāh 'creditors'); laqqāhe 'borrowing, lending'; talaqqāhi 'debtor'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. laqqaḥa 'impregnate, conceive' (Noel-Deke, ZDMG. 40[1886],723). Sar. lqh 'take', Amh. laqaḥ, Aram. lqh (itpǝ'al) 'be taken, be married'. Ph.Ug. lqh 'take', Akk. leqû. ETHIOPIC: Te. 'alaqaha (or 'alaqqaha?) 'lend', 'alaqqaha, Amh. läqqa, Har. (a)lēqaha (and alēqa, with metathesis); also in Cushitic: Or. liqē-ssa 'lend', Qab. liqē-sso, Sid. liqi-ssi, Sa. liqaha.

phonetics: laqlaqa (T) 'cover with whitewash, plaster, overlay (with metal, wood, etc)';
meaning: 'cover with whitewash, plaster, overlay (with metal, wood, etc)'; laqlāqe (K*) 'plastering, covering with whitewash'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: perhaps Ar. laqlaqa 'agitate, shake', Soq. liqeliqeq 'confuse', from a basic meaning 'mix', possibly also Ar. laqqa 'lap, lick', Heb. lāqaq, PbH. liqleq. ETHIOPIC: Te. läqlaqa 'smear over', Tna. läqlaqa, Amh. läqälläqa, Gur. läqäläqa; also in Cushitic: Bil. lalaq (from lqlq), Kham. lileq.
ETHIOPIC: Te. läqma 'gather, pluck', Tna. läqäma, Amh. läqäma, Arg. läqäma, Har. läqäma, Gur. läqäma; passed into Cushitic: Bil. laqam, Kham. liqem. See also laqama II.

phonetics: laqama II 'chew on food that is hard and makes noise when it is eaten';
meaning: 'chew on food that is hard and makes noise when it is eaten';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. laqima 'eat, swallow up'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. läqäma 'eat roasted grain', Te. läqma 'eat'. The root might also be identified with laqama I 'pick > peck, graze (as in Amharic), eat'.

phonetics: laqaqa (yǝlǝqaq) (T.Y) 'shackle, bind a prisoner to his guard, (D 1426) strike through and through';
meaning: 'shackle, bind a prisoner to his guard, (D 1426) strike through and through';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. laqqa 'strike with the hand'.

meaning: 'mourn'; 'alqaṣa (K*) 'cause to mourn'; 'alāqaṣa 'condole with anyone, show sympathy'; talāqaṣa (K*) 'be mourned'; talāqaṣa (K*) 'mourn publicly'; 'astalāqaṣa (K*) 'console'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. laqisu n-nafsi 'sad' (Dozy II 543); perhaps also Ar. mulāqiṣ 'patient, who endures a lot'; a Geez form such as 'alāqaṣa 'show sympathy, condole with someone' would mean 'help someone to endure' (Fleisch 210). ETHIOPIC: Te. läqṣa 'mourn', laqṣa 'pay a visit of condolence', Tna. 'aläqäṣa, Amh. aläqäṣa.
proper name of the apostle Luke interpreted, with reference to Latin lux, lucis, as 'light'.

phonetics: (T.Y.M) 'open the mouth, make the mouth wide open, (T.Y.M) yawn'
origin_explanation: The Amh. lägg™ä 'put on a bridle' given by T (as a translation of lagaqa) is strange since one would rather expect 'release the bridle for lagaqa'; SEMITIC: related to Ar. laqqa (lqq) 'come loose', ETHIOPIC: likewise lqq, as in Tna. lägägä 'take apart', Te. läqqa (lqq), Amh. läqäqä, Arg. läqäqa, Gur. läqäqä, Har. läqäqä 'twist out of joint'.

phonetics: larōn, läron (T) 'sea gull', (T) = Amh. yäbra 'mythical three-headed bird'; from Gk. laros, acc. laron 'seagull'. See also näron.
origin_explanation: from Gk. laros, acc. laron 'seagull'. See also näron.

SEMITIC: Ar. lasada 'suck, lick'; Heb. läšad 'pastry' does not seem to correspond to the meaning of G. lasd 'butter'. contrary to Dillmann 38 (followed by Buhl 391, Koehler 486, Baumgartner 509). Because of Tna. läsdi also occurring as läsda, variant of lästa I derived (Leslau 1958:29) Tna. läsda (and, by implication, also G. lasd) from lästa, name of a province south of Tigre following Coulbeaux-Schrei- ber 86-7: «lästa 'beurre frais qui n'a pas passé par le feu; cette dénomination lui vient de cette province: beurre de Lasta (en Amh. läsəta qäbä) parce que dans cette contrée on conserve le beurre frais, sans le faire bouillir, comme cela a lieu dans toutes les autres provinces'». One might be inclined to com- pare Akk. lišdu 'cream' (also lildu) were it not for the doubtful meaning of lišdu, according to von Soden 552, under lildu; cp., however, PBh. lašad 'fat'.

phonetics: lasha I (L) laša (T.Y.M) 'be insipid, be unseasoned, be tasteless, lose its saltiness, be flavorless, be ineffective, be inefficient, be harmless, be nonsense, be used up, be worn- out, blunder, (L.T.Y.M) fail (strength)'; 'alšaha, causative;
meaning: 'be insipid, be unseasoned, be tasteless, lose its saltiness, be flavorless, be ineffective, be inefficient, be harmless, be nonsense, be used up, be worn- out, blunder, (L.T.Y.M) fail (strength)'

phonetics: lasuh (fem. lasəht) 'insipid, flavorless, tasteless, ineffective, used up, worn-out'; lässəhe (Gr.27) 'insipidity, flatness, foolishness, nonsense'; lašhat 'insipidity, folly'; 'alšahi, malsəhi (Gr.27), part. of 'alšaha.
meaning: 'insipid, flavorless, tasteless, ineffective, used up, worn-out'
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Praetorius 1890:369 compares it with Ar. salih 'tasteless (food)', with metathesis. ETHIOPIC: perhaps related to Amh. läsä 'become tepid, be lukewarm', or G. halaşa I 'be bland, lack seasoning'.

phonetics: laša II 'smack the lips, chew saliva making noise'.
meaning: 'smack the lips, chew saliva making noise'

phonetics: lassän (T) 'tongue, language'; (lassäna sab) 'a prayer against an evil tongue'; see S. Grébaut, Aethiopica 3 (1936).6-12; Ham- merschmidt-Six 1983:138; (lassäna fətrat [Lt] 'mother tongue') (lassäna laşliš 'balance'); lassana (T) 'prattle' (denominative); talassana 'babble on, talk foolishly, prattle'; lassänäwi (K*) 'big talker';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. lisän 'tongue', Sar. ls₂n, Soq. lešin, Heb. läšōn, Aram. liššänä, Syr. leššānā, Ug. lšn, Akk. lišānu. Bittner, WZKM 23 (1909).144- 50 derives it from ls 'lick', with the suffix -än, for which he gives examples from Semitic and Cushitic. He considers it related to lḥs 'lick' (see lahasa, above). ETHIOPIC: Te. nässal 'tongue', also lässan (Brockel- mann 1908:227), Tna. Amh. läs(s)an; Te. lässana 'praise' (denominative); Tna. lässänä 'entice' could either have developed from lässänä 'polish' or be considered a denominative from lässan; passed into Cushitic: Bil. näsälä 'tongue' (from Tigre).
SEMITIC: Ar lasaqa 'agitate, shake'. ETHIOPIC: Reinisch 1887:252 compares Bil. laqač 'stagger, hesitate'.

phonetics: lasaqa (yəläsaq) (D.T.Y.M) 'be close to, draw close, adhere, cling to, be affixed'; 'aläsaqa, causative; also 'cling, be on the point of, be near to (as in 'aläsaqa la-mawit 'he was near death'), draw near, approach'; taläsaqa 'join one another, be side by side by contiguous, become one, cling, stick together, be connected, stay near, adhere to oneself, persevere'; 'astaläsaqa 'join closely, put together, unite, glue together, (Gr.493) associate oneself with someone'; läsuq 'adhering, adjoined, attached, connected, that clings, close to, near, borderline, contin- uous'; läsəq, läsq (Lt) 'connection, joining'; läsaq (läsq) 'inner place, secluded place of house)'; läsqat (Lt.TS) 'union, connection, bringing near, being near, annexation'; 'astaläsaqi, part. of 'astaläsaqa.
meaning: 'be close to, draw close, adhere, cling to, be affixed'
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. lasiqa 'adhere', Sar. (Qataban) lsq 'at- tach oneself to' (Müller), Hars. laysəq 'stick to'. ETHIOPIC: Amh. läṭṭäqä 'be fastened, be joined'. Cushitic: Be. läseq 'make stick on, paste together' borrowed from an Arabic dialect such as Yemen- ite where läsiq 'attached' occurs. See also läšäq.
SEMITIC: Ar. (Hadr) lisqa 'a kind of lizard', perhaps from lasaqa 'cling to, be stuck to'. For the develop- ment of meaning, cp. Ar. lahaka 'cleave to', and lahaka 'kind of lizard'.

phonetics: lusit I (T) = Amh. dängätär 'narrow colored stripe paralleling the wide colored stripe of a šämma-garment'.
meaning: 'narrow colored stripe paralleling the wide colored stripe of a šämma-garment'

phonetics: läsaya (L) lädaya (T) 'shave, be shaved, shave oneself'; 'aläšaya, 'aläšaya (K*) 'cause to shave, to cause one to shave oneself'; ləšuy, ləšuy (fem. ləšuyt) 'shaved'.
maläse (maläsi) 'razor, lancet'; the translation 'beehive' given by D.M. is strange;
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Tna. lasäyä 'shave', Te. lasa (lsy), Amh. Gur. laččä, Arg. lačča; also in Cushitic: Qem. Bil. liš, Kham. lič. The derivation of Ethiopic lsy from Cushitic suggested by Fleisch 206 is unlikely.

phonetics: lat (D 1399, T) 'the five books of Moses';
origin_explanation: Dillmann 1399 considers it a foreign word without giving the origin. Ambros suggests to explain lat for lädat in 'orit za-lädat 'Genesis' and used as pars pro toto.

phonetics: lat (K*) 'plant that grows on ash heaps and has a sticky sap that softens scabs'; from Amh. lat.
meaning: 'plant that grows on ash heaps and has a sticky sap that softens scabs'
origin_explanation: from Amh. lat.

phonetics: lita 'for me', loṭṭu 'for him', läṭṭi 'for her', loṭṭon 'for them'; see la.
origin_explanation: see la.

phonetics: loṭa 'for me', loṭṭu 'for him', läṭṭi 'for her', loṭṭon 'for them'; see la.
latha (T.Y.M) 'take a mouthful of liquid, (K*) blow up the cheeks' (reconstructed de- nominative from maltäht 'cheek'); 'altäha (K*), causative; maltäht (pl. malätəh) 'cheek, jaw'.
meaning: 'take a mouthful of liquid, (K*) blow up the cheeks'
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: maltäht is related to Heb. *malṭə'ōt, məṭal- 'ōt 'jawbones' (Dillmann 45). Holma 151 (following Hommel; so also Driver, JTS 23[1922].407) con- nects it with Akk. atta'u < atta'u 'teeth'. Driver derives Akk. atta'u from alta'u, from laṭa' 'lacerate'. Note that von Soden 87 does not give any transla- tion for atta'u while CAD, vol. I, II, p. 511 renders it by 'fang (?)'. Praetorius 1890:24-5 suggests Heb. ləḥi 'cheek' with an infixed t, or Ar. läḥa 'shine'. Neither of these suggestions is convincing. ETHIOPIC: Te. maltäḥ 'upper part of the cheek', Tna. mälaṭähti 'temple, side of the forehead'. Amh. mälaṭṭa 'press one's hand against one's cheek'. See also mltḥ, below.

phonetics: latama (D.T.Y) 'crush, pound, refine, grind, hull grain'; 'altama (Gr.36), causative; also 'crush, pound, beat to death'; talatma, passive; latämi (Gr.36), act. part. of latama;

origin_explanation: probably misprint for lolawa , also rendered Amh. täbbäšä 'roast' in Tayyä.

phonetics: laşlaşa (T.Y.MA.M) 'weigh, measure, be unsteady (scale), be inclined (scale), move to and fro'; 'alaşlaşa, causative; laşlaş (T) zalaslaş 'tongue of a scale, poise of a scale, pointer of a scale'; laşlaše 'fluctuation, moving to and fro, swing- ing'.
meaning: 'weigh, measure, be unsteady (scale), be inclined (scale), move to and fro'

origin_explanation: denominative from the proper name Levi.

talawaya, talāwaya, talawaya, passive; malwāy (K*) 'something that is wrapped around', that is, 'turban'. SEMITIC: Ar. lawā (lwy) 'twist', Hars. lewō 'bend', Heb. lūlā'ōt 'loops, nooses', from a reduplicated lwy (Koehler 482); it is not to be connected with G. malelīt 'joint, limb', as suggested by Baumgartner 504; Heb. also liwyā 'wreath', Akk. lawū 'wrap up'. Note that Koehler 475 and Baumgartner 496 also compare Heb. lwy 'accompany', (nif-'al) 'be joined unto', Aram.-Syr. lawā 'accompany', Md. lwa. ETHIOPIC: Tna. lāwāyā 'twist', Te. lāwla 'wind around' (from lwlw), lāwya 'crooked'; also in Cushitic: Bed. luwī 'plait'. See also lawawa, lawala.
transcription of Heb. liwyātān; also Amh. lewatan. See lewātā, lewātān.
from Aram.-Syr. lūz, lūzā (Noeldeke 1910:43); also in Te.Tna.Amh. lāwz, Har. lōz 'groundnut'. For the Aramaic origin of Ar. lawz, see Fraenkel 145; in other Semitic languages: Heb. lūz, Md. luza.

meaning: 'twist, wrap around, (K*) deviate from the road'
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. lāda (lwd) 'turn aside', Heb. nālōz (nif-'al of lwz, lāz) 'devious, crooked'.

meaning: 'scarlet, scarlet thread, (T) red dye used for dyeing cloth or fly whisks'
'wooden shovel used for winnowing the trampled grain'
origin_explanation: from Amh. layda. See also lāmedā.

meaning: 'become chief, become religious head, become an elder'
origin_explanation: (denominative from liq, above).

meaning: talayqa 'be appointed religious head or teacher'

origin_explanation: in fact it is a place name lozā, Heb. lūz identified with bet 'el 'house of God'; in Geez poetry it is identified with 'church' (see A. Grohmann, Äthiopische Marienhymnen 290, n. 1). See also lozān.
ETHIOPIC: Amh. lāzāllāzā 'throb with pain (finger)'.

meaning: 'laudanum, the resinous juice obtained from the shrub Cistus ladaniferus'
origin_explanation: Gr.39 has also lozā, luzā; so also A. Grohmann, Äthiopische Marienhymnen, p. 43, but on p. 290, n.1 he considers it a place name, for which see lozā I, above; SEMITIC: Ar. lawdanūn 'laudanum' (Dozy), ladun, lāduṇ, SAr. ldn, Syr. ladnā, Aram. luḍnā, Md. ladan, Akk. ladinnu, ladnu. Som. ladn is an Arabic loanword. For the Semitic origin of Gk. ladanon, lēda-non, see Masson 55, n. 3.
ETHIOPIC: Amh. lāzzāzā 'be somewhat moist', Gur. lāzāzā 'become flexible from being wet'; Te. lāzza lāddā 'be soft, be smooth' is probably taken from Ar. ladda 'be pleasant'.
